B.title: Section B — Short Answer I (2 marks each)

questions.q: Find √1225 using prime factorisation.
Find the smallest number by which 180 must be multiplied to make it a perfect square.
questions.ans: 180 = 2²×3²×5. Factor 5 is unpaired. Multiply by 5. Answer: 5

questions.q: Is 1000 a perfect square? Give reason.
questions.ans: No. 1000 = 2³×5³. Factor 2 appears 3 times (odd power), so it cannot be paired completely. Not a perfect square.
questions.hint: Check if all prime factors appear in pairs

questions.q: Write 81 as the sum of consecutive odd numbers.
questions.hint: n² = sum of first n odd numbers

C.title: Section C — Short Answer II (3 marks each)

questions.q: Find √1764 using the long division method.
questions.ans: Pair digits: 17|64. Largest square ≤17 is 16=4². Remainder=1, bring down 64→164. Double quotient: 8. Find d: 82×2=164. √1764 = 42.
questions.hint: Group digits in pairs from right

questions.q: Find the smallest number to be subtracted from 500 to make it a perfect square. Also find the square root of the resulting number.
questions.hint: Find the perfect square just below 500

questions.q: A school arranges 2025 students in a square array. Find the number of rows.

D.title: Section D — Long Answer (5 marks each)

questions.q: Using the long division method, find √7056. Also verify your answer.
questions.ans: Group: 70|56. Largest square ≤70 is 64=8². Remainder=6, bring down 56→656. Double quotient: 16. Try d=4: 164×4=656 ✓. √7056=84. Verify: 84²=84×84=7056 ✓.
questions.hint: Long division method for square root

questions.q: Prove that the product of two perfect squares is always a perfect square. Give two examples to verify.
questions.ans: Let m=a² and n=b². Then m×n=a²×b²=(ab)², which is a perfect square (square of ab). Example 1: 4×9=36=6² ✓. Example 2: 25×16=400=20² ✓. Hence proved.
